content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Over the last few weeks, I’ve been working on a project called OpsPilot AI, an incident command center that combines AI-assisted analysis with human approval for operational incidents.
The original idea was simple.
When production systems fail, engineers usually receive dozens of alerts at the same time. Before they can even think about fixing the problem, they have to answer questions like:

• Which alert actually matters?

• What changed?

• Is this a false alarm?

• Which service is responsible?

• What should we investigate first?
Most of that work is still manual.
I wanted to see whether an AI system could help organize this process without taking control away from engineers.
The result became OpsPilot AI.

Why I Built It
While studying AI applications and distributed systems, I noticed that many AI demos focus on generating text or answering questions.
Operational systems are different.
An incident response workflow requires structured reasoning, evidence, safety checks, and human approval.
I wasn’t interested in building another chatbot.
Instead, I wanted to create something that behaves more like an operations assistant.
Rather than saying “here is an answer,” it should say:
“Here is what I observed, here are the possible causes, here is the evidence, here is the risk, and here is what I recommend doing next.”
That distinction became the foundation of the project.

What OpsPilot AI Does
OpsPilot AI receives operational alerts and transforms them into a structured incident workflow.
Instead of presenting raw monitoring data, it organizes information into several stages.
The system performs:

• incident triage

• observability analysis

• hypothesis generation

• runbook recommendation

• remediation planning

• risk assessment

• human approval

• execution review

• postmortem generation
Each stage produces structured JSON instead of free-form text, making the output predictable and easier for the frontend to display.
The dashboard visualizes the entire lifecycle from the first alert until the incident is resolved.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Designing the Architecture
I decided to split responsibilities across multiple agents instead of relying on a single prompt.
Each agent has one job.
For example:

• Triage Agent determines the priority.

• Observability Agent summarizes signals.

• Hypothesis Agent proposes possible root causes.

• Runbook Agent recommends operational procedures.

• Risk Agent evaluates the impact of proposed actions.

• Approval Agent prepares information for human review.

• Postmortem Agent summarizes what happened after resolution.
This separation made prompt design much easier because every agent could focus on a specific task instead of trying to solve everything at once.

Building the Backend
The backend is built with FastAPI.
FastAPI made it straightforward to organize independent API endpoints while keeping the project modular.
Internally, the application maintains an incident state machine that tracks every stage of the workflow.
The frontend communicates with the backend using REST APIs, while live operational events are streamed using Server-Sent Events (SSE).
For demonstrations, I also implemented a configurable Fast Demo Mode.
This allows the complete workflow to finish within a few seconds without changing the production architecture.
Disabling the flag immediately switches back to the full AI orchestration.

Integrating Qwen
For language reasoning, I used Qwen through Alibaba Cloud DashScope.
The backend communicates using the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, which meant I could reuse existing client logic with only a few configuration changes.
Rather than asking the model for long paragraphs, I focused on structured responses.
Every agent is instructed to return JSON matching a predefined schema.
This approach reduced parsing errors and made validation much simpler.
Whenever a response does not satisfy the schema, the backend attempts an automatic repair before falling back to a safe default.
That extra validation layer turned out to be extremely useful during development.

Deploying on Alibaba Cloud
The production backend runs on Alibaba Cloud ECS.
The deployment stack is intentionally straightforward:

• Ubuntu 24.04

• Docker

• FastAPI

• Nginx

• HTTPS with Let’s Encrypt
The frontend is hosted separately on Vercel while all API requests are routed to the backend running on Alibaba Cloud.
Separating the frontend and backend made deployments much easier during development because frontend updates did not require rebuilding the backend container.

Challenges Along the Way
The biggest challenge wasn’t deployment.
It was latency.
Some AI stages, especially hypothesis generation, took noticeably longer than expected.
Initially this slowed the entire incident pipeline.
Instead of removing reasoning, I optimized prompts, limited unnecessary output, and reduced verbose explanations.
For live demonstrations I introduced Fast Demo Mode, allowing the full workflow to be shown without waiting for long AI responses while preserving the production pipeline.
Another challenge involved deployment.
Configuring Docker, Nginx, HTTPS certificates, DNS, firewall rules, and reverse proxy settings took much longer than writing the application logic.
Although none of those tasks are particularly difficult individually, getting everything to work together reliably required careful testing.

What I Learned
This project reinforced something I’ve experienced before:
Building AI applications isn’t only about choosing a model.
Most of the work happens around the model.
Validation, orchestration, deployment, monitoring, security, structured outputs, and user experience often require more engineering effort than the prompts themselves.
I also gained practical experience deploying AI services on Alibaba Cloud and designing systems where humans remain responsible for important operational decisions.

What’s Next
OpsPilot AI is still evolving.
Some of the ideas I’d like to explore next include:

• Kubernetes integration

• Prometheus metrics ingestion

• Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for operational runbooks

• Slack and Microsoft Teams integrations

• Persistent operational memory

• Predictive incident detection

• Better visualization of service dependencies

• Support for larger enterprise environments
The long-term goal isn’t autonomous incident response.
It’s helping engineers make better decisions faster while keeping humans in control.
